articles.title: Depression Might Start with Tired Brain Cells
articles.summary: Scientists found that depression in young people might be linked to how brain and blood cells use energy. They studied 18 young adults with depression and compared them to people without depression. The cells from those with depression made more energy when resting but couldn't make enough when stressed. This could explain why people with depression feel very tired and have low mood. The researchers hope this discovery helps doctors find depression earlier and create better treatments. They think this might reduce the stigma around depression because it shows it's a real physical problem, not just feeling sad.

articles.title: A New Blood Test for Alzheimer's Disease
articles.summary: Scientists have found a new way to check for Alzheimer's disease using a blood test. Alzheimer's is a brain disease that affects many older people, making it hard for them to remember things. Usually, doctors look for certain proteins in the blood or spinal fluid to diagnose it. But now, researchers at Scripps Research have discovered a different method. They look at how proteins are folded in the blood, not just how much is there. Proteins are tiny parts in our bodies that need to be folded correctly to work properly. The study showed that changes in the folding of three specific proteins in the blood can help tell if someone has Alzheimer's or mild memory problems. This test could help doctors find the disease earlier, so people can get help sooner. It was published in a science journal in 2026 and involved looking at blood samples from over 500 people.

articles.title: Chickpeas Might Be the First Food Grown on the Moon
articles.summary: Scientists are working on a cool idea to help astronauts eat on the Moon. They want to grow chickpeas, which are like little beans, in moon dirt. Moon dirt is called regolith and is different from Earth soil because it doesn't have tiny living things that help plants grow. To make it better, scientists mixed the moon dirt with worm compost, which is made by worms eating waste. They also put special fungi on the chickpea seeds to help them get nutrients and stay healthy. In tests, chickpeas grew well in mixtures with up to 75% moon dirt. This could mean astronauts might one day grow their own food on the Moon, making space missions easier and more fun.

articles.title: A Greedy Beaver Loves Snacks
articles.summary: A greedy beaver was caught twice by rangers in Northumberland because it loved eating parsnips from a trap. The rangers set up traps with fruits and vegetables to check the health of beavers and put microchips on them. They wanted to make sure the beavers were healthy, especially the baby beavers called kits. The beaver family lives in an enclosure at Wallington, and the rangers use cameras to watch them. After catching the beaver dad twice, they plan to try again in September to catch more beavers. This story shows how animals can be curious and like tasty food, and how people work to protect them.

articles.title: A Mystery Shark in the Mediterranean Sea
articles.summary: In April 2023, fishermen caught a young great white shark off the coast of Spain. This was a very rare event because great white sharks are not often seen in the Mediterranean Sea. Scientists looked at old records from 1862 to now and found that these sharks have never really left the area. They call them a "ghost" population because they are hard to spot but still live there. The shark was a juvenile, which means it was young. Finding young sharks is important because it might mean they are having babies in the Mediterranean. Great white sharks are big predators that help keep the ocean clean by eating dead animals. They are listed as Vulnerable, which means their numbers are going down, so scientists want to protect them. By learning more about these sharks, people can understand them better and not be as scared.

articles.title: NIH Director Starts Science Talks with a Non-Scientist
articles.summary: The National Institutes of Health (NIH) is starting a new series of talks called "Scientific Freedom Lectures." The director, Jay Bhattacharya, chose the first speaker, who is not a scientist but a former journalist named Matthew Ridley. Ridley has ideas that many scientists disagree with, like saying climate change might be good and that COVID-19 came from a lab, even though most evidence shows it came from animals. Bhattacharya feels his own ideas were not listened to during the pandemic, so he wants to talk about freedom in science. The article explains that this choice is surprising because the speaker does not have strong scientific support for his views, and it might not help scientists trust the talks.
